proof:
Let \(n = 2025\).  The grid consists of \(n \times n\) unit squares.  We are to place axis‑aligned rectangular tiles (whose sides lie on grid lines) such that every square is covered at most once and each row and each column contains exactly one uncovered square.  We need the minimum number of tiles.

First note that the set of uncovered squares forms a permutation matrix: one square in each row and each column.  By applying a suitable permutation of the rows and a permutation of the columns we may assume that the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal cells \((i,i)\) for \(i = 1,\dots,n\).  (The number of tiles is invariant under such relabellings, so the minimum over all permutations is attained for the diagonal case.)

**Upper bound.**  For each row \(i\) (\(1\le i\le n\)) the uncovered cell is at column \(i\).  The cells to the left of the diagonal in row \(i\) are columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\) (if \(i>1\)); these form a contiguous block and can be covered by one \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle.  Similarly, the cells to the right are columns \(i+1,\dots,n\) (if \(i<n\)) and can be covered by a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle.  Taking all these rectangles gives a covering of all off‑diagonal cells.  The number of rectangles is
\[
\underbrace{(n-1)}_{i=2,\dots,n}\;\text{(left)} \;+\; \underbrace{(n-1)}_{i=1,\dots,n-1}\;\text{(right)} \;=\; 2n-2.
\]
Hence at most \(2n-2\) tiles suffice.

**Lower bound.**  Now consider any tiling of the complement of the diagonal (the cells with \(i\neq j\)) by rectangles.  We prove that at least \(2n-2\) rectangles are needed.

Consider the upper triangle \(U = \{(i,j): i<j\}\) and the lower triangle \(L = \{(i,j): i>j\}\).  A rectangle that contains a cell from \(U\) cannot contain any cell from \(L\): if it did, the intervals of rows and columns would intersect, producing a diagonal cell, which is forbidden.  Therefore every rectangle lies entirely in \(U\) or entirely in \(L\).

For the upper triangle, look at the \(n-1\) cells
\[
S_U = \{(i,i+1): 1\le i\le n-1\}.
\]
We claim that no rectangle can contain two distinct cells of \(S_U\).  Suppose a rectangle \(R\) contains \((i,i+1)\) and \((j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\).  Because \(R\) lies in \(U\), its row interval \(I\) and column interval \(J\) satisfy \(\max I < \min J\) (otherwise a diagonal cell would appear).  Then \(i,j\in I\) and \(i+1,j+1\in J\).  Now consider the cell \((j,i+1)\).  Since \(j\in I\) and \(i+1\in J\), this cell belongs to \(R\).  Because \(i<j\), we have \(i+1\le j\).  If \(i+1=j\) then \((j,i+1)\) is the diagonal cell \((j,j)\), which is uncovered – impossible.  If \(i+1<j\) then \((j,i+1)\) lies in the lower triangle, contradicting that \(R\) is contained in \(U\).  Hence such a rectangle cannot exist.

Thus each cell of \(S_U\) must lie in a different rectangle.  Since there are \(n-1\) such cells, at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed to cover \(U\).

By symmetry, the lower triangle contains the \(n-1\) cells \((i+1,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n-1\), and the same argument shows that at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed for \(L\).

Because rectangles in \(U\) and \(L\) are disjoint, the total number of rectangles is at least \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

**Conclusion.**  For \(n=2025\) the minimum number of tiles is \(2\cdot2025-2 = 4048\).

\[
\boxed{4048}
\]
self_evaluation: The proof is complete and rigorous.  The upper bound is explicit; the lower bound uses a simple but crucial observation that a rectangle cannot contain two cells from the set \(\{(i,i+1)\}\) because it would force a diagonal or a lower‑triangle cell.  The symmetry argument and the invariance under row/column permutations are correctly justified.  All steps are logically sound.

The argument that any rectangle containing a cell from the upper triangle cannot contain a cell from the lower triangle (otherwise a diagonal cell would be covered) is sound, and the proof that no rectangle can contain two distinct cells of \(S_U\) is carefully justified.
